meta.title: CCA-F-4: Agent Reliability, MCP, and Claude Code Operations

questions.stem: A team is deciding whether every request must go through a planner, tool runner, and reviewer chain. When is the simpler design usually the better choice?

options.text: When the task is straightforward and can be answered directly without tools
options.explanation: Anthropic guidance recommends starting with the simplest pattern that meets the task and only adding agent loops when they materially help.

options.text: When the team wants to maximize orchestration overhead
options.explanation: Extra orchestration adds latency, cost, and failure points rather than value by itself.

options.text: When every request must be decomposed even if it is trivial
options.explanation: Forcing decomposition on trivial work creates unnecessary complexity.

options.text: When latency and cost are irrelevant
options.explanation: Latency and cost still matter in production design decisions.

questions.stem: A support bot must summarize a user message and answer from static policy text that is already in the prompt. Which architecture is the best fit?

options.text: A direct prompt-response workflow with no tool call
options.explanation: A direct workflow is sufficient because the task does not require tool use or autonomous multi-step planning.

options.text: A manager-worker multi-agent hierarchy
options.explanation: A hierarchy is heavier than the use case requires.

options.text: A tool loop that always calls three external systems
options.explanation: Mandatory external calls add avoidable latency and operational risk.

options.text: A peer-agent swarm that debates every answer
options.explanation: A swarm does not improve a simple deterministic lookup-and-answer task.

questions.stem: A team breaks a long task into retrieval, planning, and execution subtasks that can run independently before a final synthesis step. What is the main benefit of that design?

options.text: It can reduce context overload and let the system parallelize parts of the work
options.explanation: Task decomposition can isolate context, improve controllability, and allow parallelism when subtasks are independent.

options.text: It guarantees zero hallucinations
options.explanation: No orchestration pattern can guarantee zero hallucinations.

options.text: It removes the need for validation or monitoring
options.explanation: Validation and monitoring remain necessary for reliability.

options.text: It prevents the model from ever needing tools
options.explanation: Some decomposed tasks still need tools or retrieval.

questions.stem: An orchestrator agent sends specialized subtasks to separate research, policy, and formatting agents, then merges their outputs. Which pattern is this?

options.text: Hub-and-spoke orchestration with specialist subagents
options.explanation: This is the classic orchestrator pattern in which one controller delegates work to specialists and combines results.

options.text: Pure single-turn prompting
options.explanation: Single-turn prompting does not involve separate specialist agents.

options.text: A cache-control optimization
options.explanation: Caching is unrelated to task delegation.

options.text: A tool schema collision
options.explanation: Tool name collisions are a separate MCP design concern.

questions.stem: Claude chooses whether to call a tool partly from the tool description. What makes a tool description most useful?

options.text: Clear purpose, input expectations, and when the tool should be used
options.explanation: Good descriptions help Claude match user intent to the right tool and populate inputs correctly.

options.text: A very short name with no description
options.explanation: A name alone is usually insufficient for reliable selection.

options.text: Ambiguous wording so the model can improvise
options.explanation: Ambiguity harms invocation accuracy.

options.text: Internal implementation details that never affect invocation
options.explanation: Implementation detail is less important than intent, inputs, and side effects.

questions.stem: Which statement about tool execution in Anthropic tool use is correct?

options.text: Claude returns a structured tool call and the application or server executes the tool
options.explanation: Anthropic documents that Claude emits structured tool calls while execution happens in your application for client tools or on Anthropic-managed server tools.

options.text: Claude directly logs into external systems without any tool definition
options.explanation: Claude needs explicit tool definitions and does not autonomously bypass them.

options.text: Tool use only works when every tool is hosted by Anthropic
options.explanation: Client tools are supported; Anthropic hosting is not required for all tools.

options.text: A tool call means the final answer is already guaranteed to be correct
options.explanation: A tool call can still fail or return bad inputs, so validation is still required.

questions.stem: An MCP server needs to give Claude read-only access to a library of product documentation articles. Claude should be able to browse and read the articles but cannot trigger any function calls. Which MCP primitive is designed for this use case?

options.text: Tools — because tools are used whenever Claude needs to retrieve external content
options.explanation: Tools are designed for functions Claude can invoke to perform actions or retrieve dynamic data. Read-only structured content that does not require a function call is better exposed as a resource.

options.text: Resources — because resources expose structured, addressable content for Claude to read without invoking a function
options.explanation: MCP resources represent structured, addressable data — such as files, database records, or documentation articles — that Claude can read without triggering a function invocation. Resources are the appropriate primitive for browsable, static or semi-static content.

options.text: Prompts — because prompts inject contextual instructions alongside the content
options.explanation: Prompts are reusable instruction templates that operators attach to conversations to configure Claude's behavior. They are not designed for exposing document content.

options.text: Streams — because real-time content delivery requires a streaming primitive
options.explanation: MCP does not define a 'Streams' primitive. The three MCP primitives are tools, resources, and prompts.

questions.stem: Two MCP servers each expose a tool called `search`. What is the best design response?

options.text: Namespace or otherwise disambiguate the tools so invocation stays predictable
options.explanation: Disambiguation avoids collisions and makes selection reliable when multiple servers expose similar capabilities.

options.text: Rely on random selection between the two tools
options.explanation: Random selection is unreliable and hard to debug.

options.text: Remove every description field from both tools
options.explanation: Descriptions improve selection rather than harming it.

options.text: Force every request through both tools whether needed or not
options.explanation: Mandatory fan-out increases cost and can confuse downstream reasoning.

questions.stem: Which prompt element belongs in the system prompt rather than being repeated in every user message?

options.text: Global role, safety constraints, and output rules that should persist across turns
options.explanation: The system prompt is the right place for persistent role and policy guidance.

options.text: A one-off follow-up question from the user
options.explanation: A one-off user request belongs in the user turn.

options.text: The temporary contents of a single file upload
options.explanation: Ephemeral task data belongs in context, not durable policy instructions.

options.text: A random seed value for retries
options.explanation: Retry randomness is not a system-prompt concept.

questions.stem: A team wants more consistent structured JSON output from Claude. Which technique helps most directly?

options.text: Provide an explicit schema or format example and validate-and-retry if needed
options.explanation: Anthropic recommends making the desired format explicit and using validation loops for consistency-sensitive outputs.

options.text: Increase ambiguity so the model has freedom
options.explanation: Ambiguity reduces consistency.

options.text: Hide the expected keys until after generation
options.explanation: Delayed format disclosure works against schema adherence.

options.text: Replace all examples with unrelated prose
options.explanation: Unrelated prose examples do not anchor the required structure.

questions.stem: A prompt already contains detailed instructions. Why would a few-shot example still help?

options.text: It demonstrates the target pattern or tone in a concrete form the model can imitate
options.explanation: Few-shot examples provide an in-context pattern for formatting and reasoning style.

options.text: It disables the need for a system prompt
options.explanation: Examples complement rather than replace a good system prompt.

options.text: It increases the context window size
options.explanation: Examples consume context; they do not increase the window.

options.text: It guarantees that unsafe content will be accepted
options.explanation: Safety policies are not bypassed by examples.

questions.stem:
An automated assessment tool uses Claude to score student essay responses. When the same essay is submitted twice the tool must return the identical score both times for audit compliance.

Which API parameter configuration makes Claude's scoring fully reproducible?

options.text: Set `top_p` to 0.99 and leave `temperature` at its default value of 1.0
options.explanation: is incorrect. `top_p` of 0.99 still performs nucleus sampling from the top 99% of the probability distribution; combined with `temperature` at 1.0 (the default), Claude samples stochastically and can produce different outputs for the same input.

options.text: Set `temperature` to 0, which removes sampling randomness and causes Claude to deterministically select the highest-probability token at every step
options.explanation: is correct. Setting `temperature` to 0 eliminates all sampling randomness: Claude greedily picks the single highest-probability token at each step. This produces fully reproducible, deterministic output — the same input always yields the same score, which is required for audit compliance.

options.text: Set `max_tokens` to a fixed value to ensure every response has the same length
options.explanation: is incorrect. `max_tokens` limits response length but does not control token sampling. Claude can still produce different tokens up to that limit across repeated calls when temperature is non-zero.

options.text: Append a unique session identifier to the system prompt on each run so Claude can differentiate attempts
options.explanation: is incorrect. Injecting a unique session ID into the system prompt introduces variation per call, which is the opposite of the reproducibility goal. Claude's scoring would now depend on the injected identifier.

questions.stem: A conversation is becoming very long and expensive. What is the best first reliability move before the context window is exhausted?

options.text: Disable retrieval and grounding
options.explanation: Disabling grounding often worsens answer quality.

options.text: Summarize or compact stale context while keeping the facts needed for future turns
options.explanation: Compaction preserves important state while reducing token load.

options.text: Duplicate the entire conversation in every tool call
options.explanation: Duplication increases cost and token pressure.

options.text: Remove the latest user message
options.explanation: Removing the latest request loses the current task.

questions.stem: A team stores a compact handoff object between sessions. What problem does that solve best?

options.text: Eliminating the need to monitor tool failures
options.explanation: Tool failures still need operational handling.

options.text: Restoring relevant state without replaying the entire conversation
options.explanation: A handoff summary or state object lets a later session continue work without replaying every prior token.

options.text: Increasing model parameter count
options.explanation: State transfer does not affect model size.

options.text: Avoiding every need for user authentication
options.explanation: Authentication is a separate system concern.

questions.stem: Prompt caching is most valuable when which condition is true?

options.text: Only tool outputs are cached by the external database
options.explanation: Prompt caching is about repeated prompt segments, not only external DB tool outputs.

options.text: A large repeated prompt prefix is reused across many requests
options.explanation: Prompt caching reduces cost and latency when large prompt segments repeat across calls.

options.text: Every request uses completely different context
options.explanation: No reuse means little cache benefit.

options.text: The application never sends long prompts
options.explanation: Short prompts create less opportunity for cache savings.

questions.stem: An agent is unsure whether a fact is grounded in retrieved context or inferred. What is the safest response pattern?

options.text: Suppress every answer that includes uncertainty
options.explanation: Blank refusal is not always necessary when the system can state limits and verify.

options.text: Calibrate confidence, cite the retrieval, and ask to verify when support is weak
options.explanation: Confidence calibration and explicit verification requests reduce hallucination risk when evidence is weak.

options.text: State the guess confidently to keep the conversation moving
options.explanation: Overconfident guessing is unsafe.

options.text: Invent a source that looks authoritative
options.explanation: Fabricated sources are unacceptable.

questions.stem: A repository contains project-specific Claude Code instructions that should apply to all contributors in that repo. Which file is the most appropriate place?

options.text: A tool result artifact
options.explanation: Tool results are not durable instruction files.

options.text: A repo-level `CLAUDE.md` file
options.explanation: Claude Code uses `CLAUDE.md` files to provide persistent scoped instructions, including repo-level guidance.

options.text: A one-off slash command response
options.explanation: A slash command response is ephemeral.

options.text: A random shell history file
options.explanation: Shell history is not a supported configuration mechanism.

questions.stem: What is a practical use of Claude Code slash commands?

options.text: Replacing Git entirely
options.explanation: Git remains the version control system.

options.text: Packaging a repeatable prompt or workflow behind a short command
options.explanation: Slash commands are designed to encapsulate reusable prompts or workflows.

options.text: Changing the model weights in place
options.explanation: Slash commands do not alter model weights.

options.text: Bypassing permission controls permanently
options.explanation: They do not remove permission systems.

questions.stem: A team wants to run a shell script automatically before or after certain Claude Code tool events. Which feature is designed for that?

options.text: Prompt caching
options.explanation: Prompt caching does not trigger shell scripts.

options.text: Hooks
options.explanation: Hooks exist for pre-tool and post-tool automation around Claude Code workflows.

options.text: Few-shot prompting
options.explanation: Few-shot prompting changes model behavior, not tool event automation.

options.text: Temperature tuning
options.explanation: Temperature tuning changes randomness.

questions.stem: Why would a team use isolated worktrees when running parallel Claude Code tasks?

options.text: To guarantee that tool permissions are never needed
options.explanation: Permissions remain a separate control.

options.text: To reduce branch conflicts and keep separate task contexts isolated
options.explanation: Separate worktrees help parallel tasks avoid stomping on each other while preserving independent task context.

options.text: To make every task share the same uncommitted files
options.explanation: Sharing a single mutable working tree increases conflicts.

options.text: To disable version control history
options.explanation: Worktrees are still part of Git history.

questions.stem:
An orchestrator spawns four parallel research agents. Each agent calls a `search_web` tool. After all agents complete, the orchestrator aggregates their results. Two agents return `{"results": [...]}` but two others return `{"data": [...]}`. The aggregation step raises a `KeyError` when it tries to read `result["results"]` from agents that used `"data"`.

What is the root cause of this inconsistency?

options.text: Parallel tool calls always produce different key names because each agent receives a separate context window
options.explanation: is incorrect. Separate context windows do not cause key-name variation. Each agent's context is independent, but the tool definition is shared — if the tool return schema is consistent, all agents see the same structure.

options.text: The `search_web` tool does not define a strict, versioned return schema — different agents interpret or fill the response structure differently
options.explanation: is correct. When a tool lacks a strict, versioned JSON return schema, different executions or implementations of the tool can use different key names (`"results"` vs `"data"`). The fix is to define an explicit output schema in the tool definition so every caller receives a guaranteed structure.

options.text: Sequential execution instead of parallel would have forced all agents to use the same response format
options.explanation: is incorrect. Sequential execution does not change the tool's return schema. If the schema is inconsistent, sequential agents encounter the same inconsistency one at a time rather than in parallel.

options.text: Each agent requires a distinct system prompt that specifies which key name to expect in the tool response
options.explanation: is incorrect. System prompt instructions to agents about which keys to expect work around a structural problem rather than fixing it. Adding per-agent prompt patches does not guarantee the tool actually returns the expected key.

questions.stem:
A pipeline calls a `lookup_inventory` tool. The tool is unavailable and returns an error. Claude's API response includes a tool result block with the error message but without a special flag. Claude ignores the error and attempts to proceed as if the lookup succeeded.

What should the tool result include to signal to Claude that an error occurred?

options.text: Set `type: "error"` in the tool result block alongside the error message
options.explanation: `type: "error"` is not the tool result field used to signal errors. Tool results use `is_error` for this purpose.

options.text: Return an empty string — Claude infers an error from missing content
options.explanation: An empty string does not signal an error condition — Claude cannot distinguish an empty success from a silent failure.

options.text: Set `is_error: true` in the tool result block so Claude knows to handle it as an error rather than a successful result
options.explanation: The `is_error: true` field in a tool result block explicitly signals to Claude that the tool call failed. Claude then knows to handle the result as an error — it can retry, use a fallback, or report the failure to the user — rather than treating the error message as a successful result.

options.text: Include the word 'ERROR' at the start of the content string — Claude detects this keyword
options.explanation: Claude does not detect errors by scanning the content string for keywords like 'ERROR'. The `is_error` field is the designated mechanism.

questions.stem:
A pipeline generates a purchase order and then sends it to a payment processor. Before Claude calls the `submit_payment` tool, the orchestrator should perform a final check.

Which check is the most important safety gate?

options.text: Verify that the purchase order JSON is valid (parses without errors)
options.explanation: Valid JSON syntax does not mean the payment details (amount, recipient) are correct.

options.text: Check that Claude's confidence score for the payment is above 0.9
options.explanation: Claude does not produce a 'confidence score' for tool calls in the Anthropic API.

options.text: Confirm with a human approver that the amount, recipient, and payment reference are correct before submitting
options.explanation: Submitting a payment is an irreversible action with financial consequences. Before executing, the human-in-the-loop principle requires a human approver to verify the critical fields — amount, recipient, and reference — are correct. This is the highest-value safety check.

options.text: Run the payment through a staging environment to test the tool call
options.explanation: A staging environment test may process a real payment if the tool is not properly isolated, and does not confirm the details are correct for the actual intended payment.

questions.stem:
A team runs 10,000 independent document classification tasks. Processing time is not critical — results are needed by the next morning. Using the synchronous Messages API takes 8 hours and costs $200.

Which alternative reduces both cost and allows the team to submit all 10,000 tasks at once?

options.text: Use streaming to receive classifications as they are generated, reducing wait time
options.explanation: Streaming delivers tokens faster within a single request but does not batch multiple requests or reduce per-token cost.

options.text: Chain all 10,000 documents into a single Messages API request to reduce overhead
options.explanation: Concatenating 10,000 documents would exceed context window limits and is not how the Messages API works.

options.text: Submit all 10,000 requests via the Message Batches API, which offers 50% off input and output token pricing for asynchronous batch processing
options.explanation: The Message Batches API is designed for large volumes of independent, asynchronous tasks. The 50% token pricing discount directly reduces cost (from $200 to ~$100), and all 10,000 tasks can be submitted in one batch call.

options.text: Use a smaller model for classification to reduce cost and processing time
options.explanation: A smaller model changes output quality, not just cost. The Batches API provides cost reduction while keeping the same model.

questions.stem:
A pipeline uses Claude to generate structured output in a custom XML format. The output must end with `</report>`. Claude sometimes adds text after the closing tag.

Which API parameter ensures Claude stops generating immediately after `</report>`?

options.text: Use a PostToolUse hook to trim any text after the closing tag
options.explanation: Post-processing in a hook works but is more fragile than the built-in stop_sequences mechanism.

options.text: Add 'Do not write anything after </report>' to the system prompt
options.explanation: Text instructions reduce but do not eliminate post-tag content — Claude may still occasionally add a summary after the tag.

options.text: Set `stop_sequences: ["</report>"]` in the API request so Claude halts when it produces that string
options.explanation: `stop_sequences` is the API parameter designed for this purpose. When Claude produces the specified string, generation halts immediately. This is a hard boundary enforced at the API level, not by Claude's instruction-following.

options.text: Set temperature to 0 so Claude generates shorter, more predictable output
options.explanation: Temperature 0 makes output deterministic but does not stop generation at a specific string.

questions.stem:
A data extraction system prompt describes what the model SHOULD do but includes no examples of common extraction errors to avoid. Extraction quality is 82%.

Which prompt addition is most likely to improve accuracy on the most common error types?

options.text: Remove the 'should do' instructions and rely only on examples
options.explanation: Removing positive instructions leaves Claude without the full specification of what good extraction looks like.

options.text: Ask Claude to 'double-check each field before returning' at the end of the prompt
options.explanation: 'double-check' is a meta-instruction that does not give Claude additional information about what to check for.

options.text: Add more detail to the 'should do' description so the instructions are longer
options.explanation: Longer 'should do' instructions may not address the specific failure modes — Claude already follows the positive instructions 82% of the time.

options.text: Add negative examples (showing wrong extraction with an annotation explaining why it is wrong) for each of the top error types
options.explanation: Negative examples directly target the known failure modes. Showing a wrong extraction alongside an explanation of why it is wrong provides Claude with a reference for the exact patterns to avoid, calibrating it on the specific error types the team has identified.

questions.stem:
A user asks a financial assistant: 'Should I invest in the tech sector?' The system prompt instructs Claude to provide financial information but not personalized investment advice.

What is the most appropriate response structure?

options.text: Provide a general overview of the tech sector and explain that personalized investment advice requires a licensed financial advisor
options.explanation: The response stays within the system prompt's scope (providing general financial information) while acknowledging the limitation (personalized advice requires a licensed professional). This is helpful, honest, and compliant with the system prompt constraints.

options.text: Refuse to answer and redirect the user to a different platform
options.explanation: Refusing entirely and redirecting is unnecessarily restrictive — the system prompt allows general information, which is valuable to the user.

options.text: Provide a personalized investment recommendation since the user's intent is clear
options.explanation: Providing personalized investment advice violates the explicit system prompt constraint. The system prompt constraint must be honoured.

options.text: Ask 10 clarifying questions before any response to ensure the advice is personalized
options.explanation: 10 clarifying questions without any initial response is poor user experience and does not address the user's immediate question.

questions.stem:
A math tutoring assistant needs to solve problems that require multiple sequential reasoning steps. Users report that Claude sometimes skips steps and arrives at wrong answers.

Which prompting technique most directly improves step-by-step accuracy?

options.text: Use a higher temperature for more creative problem-solving approaches
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness — the opposite of what is needed for accurate multi-step arithmetic.

options.text: Show the final answer in the system prompt and ask Claude to verify it
options.explanation: Showing the final answer and asking for verification does not produce correct step-by-step working.

options.text: Increase max_tokens to give Claude more space for each answer
options.explanation: Max_tokens grants output space but does not instruct Claude to use that space for explicit step-by-step reasoning.

options.text: Add chain-of-thought guidance: 'Work through the problem step by step, showing each calculation before stating the final answer'
options.explanation: Chain-of-thought prompting explicitly instructs Claude to externalise its reasoning process. Showing each calculation step before stating the final answer forces Claude to work through the problem in a verifiable way, reducing errors caused by implicit leaps.

questions.stem:
A developer wants to view the transcript of their current Claude Code session — all the messages, tool calls, and responses — to debug an unexpected behaviour.

Where does Claude Code store the session transcript by default?

options.text: In the terminal's scroll-back buffer — there is no persistent transcript file
options.explanation: Claude Code does persist session transcripts beyond the terminal buffer.

options.text: In `~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/<session-id>.jsonl` — each session is logged as a JSON lines file
options.explanation: Claude Code stores session transcripts in `~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/` as JSONL files. Each session has its own file containing all messages, tool calls, and results — useful for debugging, auditing, or resuming sessions.

options.text: In the project root as `claude-session.log`
options.explanation: Claude Code does not create a `claude-session.log` file in the project root.

options.text: Claude Code does not store transcripts — sessions are ephemeral
options.explanation: Claude Code does store transcripts persistently in the user's home directory.

questions.stem:
A team wants to enforce that every Python file Claude Code creates in their project must pass a Black formatter check before being committed. Currently, Claude Code sometimes writes Python that fails Black.

Which mechanism enforces this automatically without requiring Claude Code to check manually?

options.text: Configure Black as a pre-commit hook in the git configuration — Claude Code will respect git hooks
options.explanation: Git pre-commit hooks run when the developer commits, not immediately when Claude Code writes a file. They would catch the error too late in the workflow.

options.text: Ask Claude Code to run `black .` at the end of every session
options.explanation: Asking Claude Code to run Black at session end requires remembering to do so and does not catch individual file problems as they are created.

options.text: Add 'Always format Python files with Black before finishing' to CLAUDE.md
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md guidance instructs Claude but does not deterministically enforce the formatting check — Claude may forget or omit it.

options.text: Create a PostToolUse hook that runs `black --check` on every file written by Claude Code's Write or Edit tool, returning non-zero exit code if formatting fails
options.explanation: A PostToolUse hook runs automatically after every Write or Edit tool call. When `black --check` exits non-zero (formatting fails), Claude Code receives the error and can re-format the file before continuing. This is deterministic and automatic.

questions.stem:
A company has three internal teams using Claude Code: frontend (React), backend (Go), and data science (Python). Each team has different code style conventions, linting tools, and preferred test commands.

What is the most maintainable way to configure team-specific conventions for each project?

options.text: One global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` listing all three teams' conventions
options.explanation: Combining all three teams' conventions in one global file creates a long, potentially conflicting instruction set that applies everywhere, even when irrelevant.

options.text: Each project has its own CLAUDE.md at the project root with that team's conventions
options.explanation: Project-level CLAUDE.md files scope instructions to the relevant project. The React project's CLAUDE.md lists ESLint and npm test; the Go project's lists golangci-lint and go test; the Python project's lists Black and pytest. Developers work in the right context automatically.

options.text: Each developer maintains personal CLAUDE.md files with their individual preferences
options.explanation: Personal files apply the same individual preferences to all projects regardless of team context.

options.text: All conventions are communicated to Claude Code through user messages at the start of each session
options.explanation: Repeating conventions in user messages every session is error-prone and not scalable.

questions.stem:
A developer wants Claude Code to always use `claude-opus-4-8` for a specific project without passing `--model` on every invocation. The setting should apply to the project only, not to the developer's other projects.

Where should this configuration be stored?

options.text: In the `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` environment variable set in the system shell profile (`~/.zshrc`)
options.explanation: is incorrect. Setting `ANTHROPIC_MODEL` in the shell profile applies globally to every project in that shell environment, not just the one project. It also leaks the setting to other tools that read the same environment variable.

options.text: In the project's `.claude/settings.json` file under the `"model"` key — project-level settings override user-level defaults for that directory
options.explanation: is correct. `.claude/settings.json` is the project-level settings file for Claude Code. A `"model"` key there overrides the user-level default (`~/.claude.json`) for any Claude Code session started inside that project directory. This gives per-project model control without affecting other projects.

options.text: In the project `CLAUDE.md` file under a `## Model:` heading
options.explanation: is incorrect. `CLAUDE.md` is a memory/context file that Claude reads for project-specific instructions, not a configuration file for Claude Code's runtime behaviour such as which model to use.

options.text: In a `.env` file at the project root — Claude Code reads dotenv files automatically at startup
options.explanation: is incorrect. Claude Code does not automatically parse `.env` files for API or model configuration. Model selection is controlled through the settings hierarchy (`~/.claude.json` and `.claude/settings.json`), not environment variable files.

questions.stem:
An MCP server for an e-commerce platform needs to give Claude access to product pricing. The pricing data changes every 10 minutes and Claude should always see the current price. Claude needs to read prices but must never modify them.

Which MCP primitive is most appropriate?

options.text: System prompt — include the full price list in the system prompt and update it every 10 minutes
options.explanation: Updating the system prompt every 10 minutes requires application-level coordination outside the MCP protocol and is not scalable.

options.text: MCP Tool — define a `get_price` function that returns current pricing data when called
options.explanation: A Tool invocation has no side effects here but works — however, it requires Claude to actively poll for price updates, which is less efficient than server-pushed updates for frequently changing data.

options.text: MCP Resource with subscription support — expose each product's price as a subscribable resource that pushes updates when prices change
options.explanation: MCP Resources with subscription support are designed for read-only, addressable data that changes over time. The server pushes notifications when prices change, allowing Claude to see current pricing without polling — and the read-only nature matches the requirement that Claude must never modify prices.

options.text: MCP Prompt — embed current pricing in a prompt template that is re-generated every 10 minutes
options.explanation: Prompt templates are injected once at conversation start and do not update dynamically during a session.

questions.stem:
A developer builds an MCP tool `generate_image` that returns an image file. The tool result content type must be set correctly for Claude to process the image inline.

What content type should the tool result use for binary image data?

options.text: Return the image as a base64-encoded string with content type `text/plain`
options.explanation: Base64 with `text/plain` content type prevents the client from recognising the data as an image.

options.text: Return the image as a URL string with content type `text/plain` and let Claude fetch it separately
options.explanation: Returning a URL requires Claude to make a separate fetch, and the tool result itself would not contain the image data for inline processing.

options.text: Return the image as a base64-encoded blob with content type `image/png` (or appropriate MIME type)
options.explanation: MCP tool results support multiple content types including image content. Returning a base64-encoded blob with the correct MIME type (e.g., `image/png`) allows the client to render and process the image inline.

options.text: MCP tools can only return text — image data must be returned as a file path
options.explanation: MCP tools can return image content — they are not limited to text only.

questions.stem:
A workflow assistant MCP server needs to provide Claude with a standardised checklist template for conducting quarterly business reviews. The checklist should be available as an invokable template that Claude can request to begin a structured QBR session.

Which MCP primitive is the best fit?

options.text: MCP Tool — define a `start_qbr_checklist` function that returns the template text
options.explanation: A Tool invocation implies a function with potential side effects. Using a tool to return a static template is technically possible but semantically incorrect — there are no side effects.

options.text: MCP Resource — expose the template as a document at a stable URI for Claude to fetch
options.explanation: A Resource is addressable read-only data, typically fetched when needed. It is not designed to inject structured conversation context at invocation time.

options.text: MCP Prompt — define the QBR checklist as a reusable prompt template that injects structured context into the conversation
options.explanation: MCP Prompts are designed exactly for this use case — reusable conversation templates that inject structured instructions when invoked. The QBR checklist template becomes a standard workflow that Claude can invoke to begin a structured session.

options.text: System prompt — embed the full QBR template in the application's system prompt
options.explanation: Embedding in the application's system prompt couples the template to the application code and makes it unavailable through the standardised MCP interface.

questions.stem:
An MCP tool submits purchase orders to an ERP system and returns one of four error types: temporary network timeouts, invalid field values in the order, insufficient inventory, or the calling user lacking purchase authorisation. An orchestrating agent handles each failure.

Which handling strategy correctly maps each error category to the appropriate response?

options.text: Retry all errors up to three times; escalate to a human operator if all retries fail
options.explanation: Retrying all error types wastes resources on permanent failures. Retrying an invalid-field error or a permission error will always fail — the root cause (bad input or missing auth) must be resolved before any retry can succeed.

options.text: Classify by HTTP status code: 5xx → retry; 4xx → notify the user; 3xx → follow redirect
options.explanation: HTTP status codes are a transport-layer concern not exposed directly in MCP tool results. MCP error semantics are communicated through structured error response fields, not HTTP codes. Additionally, HTTP code-based routing cannot distinguish a business logic error from a permission error, both of which may surface as 403 or 422 in underlying APIs.

options.text: Transient errors (network timeout) → retry with backoff; validation errors (invalid fields) → correct the input and retry; business logic errors (insufficient inventory) → escalate to a procurement workflow; permission errors (missing authorisation) → stop and notify an authorised user
options.explanation: MCP error responses distinguish four categories that require four distinct handling strategies: transient errors are safe to retry; validation errors require input correction before retry; business logic errors indicate a workflow issue requiring escalation; permission errors indicate an authorisation gap requiring human intervention. Matching the handling to the category prevents futile retries and unnecessary escalations.

options.text: Log all errors and continue processing subsequent orders — error handling interrupts throughput
options.explanation: Silently continuing after an error produces a misleading result: the order was not submitted, but the pipeline continues as if it was. Errors must be handled explicitly, not swallowed.

questions.stem:
A multimodal pipeline processes contracts that each include 3 page images (each ~500 tokens) and 1,500 tokens of text. The model has a 200,000-token context window.

Approximately how many contracts can be processed in a single API call?

options.text: Approximately 10 contracts (text only; images are not counted against the context window)
options.explanation: Images DO consume context window tokens. Images are not free — they are converted to a token count based on their dimensions and detail level.

options.text: Approximately 133 contracts (200,000 ÷ 1,500 tokens each)
options.explanation: This calculation ignores the image tokens. Each contract is 1,500 text + 3 × 500 image = 3,000 tokens total.

options.text: Approximately 67 contracts (200,000 ÷ 3,000 tokens per contract: 1,500 text + 3 × 500 image)
options.explanation: The full token budget per contract is 1,500 (text) + 1,500 (3 images × 500 each) = 3,000 tokens. The 200,000-token window accommodates approximately 200,000 ÷ 3,000 ≈ 67 contracts (with some budget reserved for instructions and output).

options.text: Unlimited — the model compresses images before counting them
options.explanation: The model does not compress images before counting tokens — each image contributes its full token cost to the context window.

questions.stem:
A long-running customer support conversation has accumulated 50 turns. The earliest turns cover an issue that was resolved 40 turns ago. The agent now has a new unrelated issue to address.

Which context management approach preserves relevant context while reducing token count?

options.text: Keep all 50 turns — prior context may contain useful background information
options.explanation: Keeping all 50 turns grows context quadratically across sessions and keeps irrelevant resolved content competing with the current issue.

options.text: Discard all context older than 10 turns without summarising
options.explanation: Silently discarding older turns without summarisation loses information that may have introduced constraints or decisions relevant to the current interaction.

options.text: Summarise the resolved issue into a 1-2 sentence summary and remove the 40 turns of detail, keeping the summary and the last 10 turns in context
options.explanation: Summarising the resolved issue into a brief note preserves the key facts (resolution, commitments made) while removing the verbose detail. The last 10 turns provide recent context. This compresses without information loss.

options.text: Start a completely new conversation without carrying any prior context
options.explanation: Discarding all prior context loses any account-level context, user preferences, or commitments made in earlier turns.

questions.stem:
A developer plans an agentic task that will take approximately 100 tool calls. Each tool response averages 1,000 tokens. The system prompt is 5,000 tokens. The model has a 100,000-token context window.

Which context management strategy must be in place before the task begins?

options.text: No strategy needed — 100 × 1,000 + 5,000 = 105,000 tokens fits in the 100,000-token window
options.explanation: 105,000 tokens exceeds the 100,000-token window — the task will fail without a strategy. Also, each turn adds both a tool call and the model response, making the actual context growth higher than estimated.

options.text: Plan to start a new session after every 50 tool calls
options.explanation: Starting a new session after 50 calls loses the accumulated task state needed for the remaining 50 calls.

options.text: Implement progressive summarisation: periodically compress older tool results into summaries to keep total context under the window limit throughout the task
options.explanation: Progressive summarisation compresses older tool results into condensed summaries as the context grows, keeping the running total within the window throughout the task. This is the standard strategy for long agentic runs.

options.text: Increase max_tokens to expand the context window to 150,000 tokens
options.explanation: Max_tokens controls output length, not input context capacity. The context window size is a model property, not a request parameter.

questions.stem:
A Claude-based contract extraction system returns a `confidence` score (0.0–1.0) with each field extraction. Before deploying to production, the team wants to verify that extractions with high confidence scores are actually more accurate than those with low scores — i.e., that the confidence scores are well-calibrated.

Which evaluation approach directly tests whether confidence scores predict accuracy?

options.text: Calculate the mean confidence score across all test documents — a mean above 0.8 indicates reliable extraction
options.explanation: Mean confidence measures self-reported certainty, not calibration accuracy. A model that assigns 0.85 confidence to every extraction — correct and incorrect alike — has a high mean but is completely uncalibrated.

options.text: Manually verify 10 randomly selected extractions and check that the verified-correct ones have a higher mean confidence than the overall mean
options.explanation: 10 randomly selected extractions is an insufficient sample size, and a non-stratified sample cannot reveal whether accuracy varies across the confidence range. This approach cannot demonstrate that 0.9-confidence extractions are more accurate than 0.4-confidence ones.

options.text: Sort all extractions by confidence score, divide them into quartiles, and manually verify samples from each quartile — calibration is confirmed if accuracy increases consistently from the lowest to the highest quartile
options.explanation: Stratified sampling by confidence quartile is the standard calibration evaluation method. By verifying accuracy within each quartile and observing whether it increases from Q1 (lowest confidence) to Q4 (highest confidence), the team directly tests whether the confidence scores are predictive of accuracy. If all quartiles show the same accuracy, the scores are not discriminative and the threshold approach in option D would be unreliable.

options.text: Deploy with a 0.7 confidence threshold and monitor post-deployment error rates — calibration testing should happen in production with real data
options.explanation: Deploying without pre-deployment calibration testing cannot establish whether the chosen threshold is sound. If confidence scores are uncalibrated, the 0.7 threshold rejects valid extractions and passes invalid ones at roughly equal rates. Calibration must be verified before, not after, deployment.

questions.stem: A Claude API application calls Claude with tools. The API response contains `stop_reason: "tool_use"`. What does this indicate and what should the application do next?

options.text: Claude has finished the task; extract the final answer from the response content
options.explanation: `stop_reason: "tool_use"` explicitly signals that Claude is not done — it is waiting for tool results.

options.text: An error occurred during tool processing; retry the request
options.explanation: `tool_use` stop reason is not an error; it is a normal part of the agentic loop.

options.text: Claude wants to invoke one or more tools; extract the tool_use blocks, execute them, and return the results as tool_result messages
options.explanation: `stop_reason: "tool_use"` means Claude has generated tool_use content blocks requesting one or more tool invocations. The application must execute those tools and return the results as `tool_result` blocks before Claude can continue.

options.text: The context window is full; summarise the conversation and start a new session
options.explanation: `tool_use` is unrelated to context window limits.

questions.stem:
An agentic loop runs until Claude returns `stop_reason: "end_turn"`. After 200 iterations, the loop is still running because Claude keeps returning `tool_use` stop reasons, but all tool calls are returning empty results.

What is the most likely problem and the correct fix?

options.text: The max_tokens is too low — increase it so Claude can finish the task in one response
options.explanation: Max_tokens affects response length, not the number of tool call iterations.

options.text: The agent lacks a termination condition; add a maximum iteration count and break the loop when it is reached
options.explanation: An agentic loop without a maximum iteration guard can run indefinitely. Adding a cap (e.g., 50 iterations) and breaking with an appropriate message when reached is the standard safeguard against runaway loops.

options.text: The tool_use blocks are malformed; validate them before execution
options.explanation: Malformed tool_use blocks would produce API errors, not repeated empty results.

options.text: Empty tool results are not valid; return an error to Claude instead of an empty result
options.explanation: Empty results are valid tool outcomes. An empty search result is different from an error. The problem is the loop, not the result format.

questions.stem:
A financial reporting pipeline uses a hub-and-spoke architecture. The hub (coordinator) receives a request for a quarterly report and dispatches subtasks to a data-retrieval agent, a calculation agent, and a formatting agent.

What is the coordinator's primary responsibility in this architecture?

options.text: Execute the tool calls that each spoke agent requests
options.explanation: Spoke agents execute their own tool calls. The coordinator orchestrates, not executes.

options.text: Break down the goal into subtasks, route each to the appropriate spoke, and synthesise the results into the final output
options.explanation: In a hub-and-spoke architecture the coordinator's role is: decompose the goal into discrete subtasks, dispatch each subtask to the appropriate specialist agent, and synthesise the returned results into the final deliverable. This is the defining function of the hub.

options.text: Provide the system prompt for each spoke agent based on the report type
options.explanation: System prompt generation for spoke agents is a configuration concern, not a runtime coordination responsibility.

options.text: Validate that the spoke agents' outputs conform to the required JSON schema
options.explanation: While output validation is a sound practice, it is a secondary responsibility. The primary role is decomposition and synthesis.

questions.stem:
A team deploys a multi-agent system where each subagent is given a copy of the full system state at invocation. The state includes 50KB of configuration data, 30KB of user history, and 10KB of task-specific context.

Which context design reduces cost and latency without sacrificing task performance?

options.text: Give every subagent all 90KB so no information is lost
options.explanation: Providing all 90KB to every subagent increases input token cost proportional to the number of agents without improving task performance if each agent only uses a fraction.

options.text: Give each subagent only the task-specific context it needs for its assigned subtask
options.explanation: Scoping each subagent's context to what it actually needs reduces input tokens and cost while maintaining full task performance. A formatting agent does not need user history; a retrieval agent does not need configuration data.

options.text: Compress the 90KB state to a 1KB summary and give all subagents the same summary
options.explanation: Aggressive compression may discard information that some subagents need, harming task accuracy.

options.text: Store all 90KB in the system prompt and use prompt caching to reduce cost
options.explanation: Prompt caching helps when the same content is reused across calls, but does not reduce the per-agent cost of sending the same 90KB to multiple agents simultaneously.

questions.stem:
A coding agent writes code, runs tests, observes failures, and revises the code in a loop. After six iterations, all tests pass. The agent is about to commit 47 changed files.

Which action best reflects safe agentic practice before committing?

options.text: Commit immediately — the passing tests prove correctness
options.explanation: Tests passing does not guarantee no unintended side effects — tests can be deleted or disabled as an easy way to 'pass'.

options.text: Run the linter on the changed files and fix any warnings before committing
options.explanation: Linting checks style, not whether the agent made semantically unintended changes like disabling assertions.

options.text: Review the diff for unintended changes (deleted tests, disabled assertions, hardcoded values) before committing 47 files
options.explanation: Reviewing the diff for unintended changes — deleted tests, disabled assertions, hardcoded values that make tests pass trivially — is the critical safety check before a large multi-file commit. This is especially important after many iteration cycles where an agent might have taken shortcuts.

options.text: Ask the user to manually approve every changed file individually before committing
options.explanation: Manual per-file approval of 47 files is impractical. A diff review is the appropriate checkpoint.

questions.stem:
A team configures a PostToolUse hook in Claude Code that runs after every Write tool call. The hook validates that the written file passes `eslint`. If the lint check fails, the hook exits non-zero.

What happens when the hook exits non-zero?

options.text: The file write is rolled back and Claude receives an error message to fix the linting issues
options.explanation: A PostToolUse hook exiting non-zero signals Claude Code that the tool's result should be treated as a failure. Claude receives the hook's stderr as an error message and can use it to diagnose and fix the linting issues before continuing.

options.text: Claude Code terminates the session and reports a critical failure
options.explanation: A hook failure does not terminate the session — it provides feedback to Claude for correction.

options.text: The hook output is logged but the file write proceeds — PostToolUse hooks cannot block writes
options.explanation: PostToolUse hooks do affect Claude's behaviour when they exit non-zero — the non-zero exit is explicitly how hooks communicate failures back to Claude.

options.text: The hook result is ignored because Claude Code does not support PostToolUse hooks
options.explanation: PostToolUse is a supported hook event in Claude Code.

questions.stem:
An orchestrator agent assigns a task to a subagent, which returns a result claiming it found 500 matching records. The orchestrator passes this result directly to a report-generation agent without verification. Later, the report is found to contain fabricated record details.

Which design flaw enabled the fabricated data to reach the report?

options.text: The subagent had too many tools, causing it to call the wrong one
options.explanation: Tool count is unrelated to output verification.

options.text: The orchestrator trusted the subagent's output without cross-checking it against the underlying data source
options.explanation: The orchestrator passed through an unverified claim (500 matching records) to the downstream agent. A well-designed orchestrator validates subagent outputs — checking record counts against actual data queries, not just accepting self-reported numbers — before propagating results.

options.text: The report-generation agent lacked sufficient context about what constitutes valid data
options.explanation: The report-generation agent's context is not the source of the problem. The fabricated data came from the first subagent's unverified claim.

options.text: The task decomposition was incorrect — report generation should be a subagent of the data-retrieval agent
options.explanation: Task decomposition structure does not cause fabrication — the lack of output verification does.

questions.stem:
A document analysis pipeline makes three sequential Claude API calls for every input document: the first extracts named entities, the second classifies them by type, and the third generates a structured summary. All three calls share an identical 900-token system prompt describing the document schema and output format. The pipeline processes 8,000 documents per day.

Which change most directly reduces per-document token cost and latency?

options.text: Combine all three steps into a single Claude call using a longer, combined prompt
options.explanation: is incorrect. Merging three steps into one call may reduce overhead but does not reduce the per-call token cost of the shared system prompt — those 900 tokens are still counted in full on every combined call.

options.text: Add `cache_control` with `type: "ephemeral"` to the system prompt so its tokens are written to the prompt cache on the first call and read from cache on subsequent calls within the same session
options.explanation: is correct. When `cache_control` is set on the system prompt, the first call writes the prompt tokens to the prompt cache. Subsequent calls that include the same system prompt hit the cache and are charged at the read rate (roughly 10% of the write rate), reducing both cost and latency substantially for a high-volume pipeline.

options.text: Switch from sequential to parallel execution using the Batches API so all three steps run simultaneously
options.explanation: is incorrect. The Batches API processes requests asynchronously and at a discount, but it does not eliminate the per-call system prompt token cost. Each batch request still counts the system prompt tokens in full unless caching is also used.

options.text: Reduce the system prompt to under 100 tokens to minimise the per-call overhead
options.explanation: is incorrect. Shortening the system prompt trades accuracy and schema coverage for cost savings. It does not eliminate the repeated per-call token overhead — it merely makes each repetition cheaper by reducing content, not by caching.

questions.stem:
A developer adds a CLAUDE.md at `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` with the line 'Always use British English spelling.' A project at `/work/api/` has its own CLAUDE.md with 'Use American English spelling.'

Which instruction takes precedence when Claude Code is running inside `/work/api/`?

options.text: The user-level `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` — user settings always override project settings
options.explanation: User-level CLAUDE.md provides global defaults, but project-level instructions override them for that specific project.

options.text: The project-level CLAUDE.md — more specific, closer-scope instructions take precedence over global ones
options.explanation: CLAUDE.md instructions follow a hierarchical override: project-level and subdirectory-level instructions are more specific and take precedence over the global user-level CLAUDE.md. American English would be used inside `/work/api/`.

options.text: Neither — conflicting instructions cancel each other and Claude uses its default
options.explanation: Conflicting instructions do not cancel — the more specific (project-level) instruction wins.

options.text: Both — Claude applies both spellings randomly depending on the turn
options.explanation: Claude does not randomly alternate between competing instructions.

questions.stem: A team defines a project-level slash command at `.claude/commands/deploy.md`. A developer also has a user-level command with the same name at `~/.claude/commands/deploy.md`. When the developer types `/deploy`, which command runs?

options.text: Both commands run in sequence — project first, then user
options.explanation: Commands with the same name do not both run — one takes precedence.

options.text: The user-level command — user commands have higher priority
options.explanation: Project-level commands are more specific to the current working context and take precedence over user-level commands with the same name.

options.text: The project-level command — project scope is more specific and takes precedence
options.explanation: Project-level slash commands defined in `.claude/commands/` take precedence over user-level commands with the same name, following the same scope hierarchy as CLAUDE.md files.

options.text: Claude Code prompts the developer to choose which one to run
options.explanation: Claude Code does not prompt for selection — precedence is determined by scope hierarchy.

questions.stem:
A team wants to add Claude Code to their CI pipeline to automatically generate release notes from merged PR descriptions. The pipeline passes the PR data via stdin and needs the output written to `RELEASE_NOTES.md` without any interactive prompts.

Which command invocation is correct?

options.text: `claude --batch` with the PR data file as an argument
options.explanation: `--batch` is not a recognised Claude Code flag for CI mode.

options.text: `claude --ci-mode` piped from stdin with stdout redirected to the file
options.explanation: `--ci-mode` is not a recognised Claude Code flag.

options.text: Pipe the PR data to `claude -p 'Generate release notes from these PR descriptions'` and redirect stdout to `RELEASE_NOTES.md`
options.explanation: The `-p` (print) flag runs Claude Code non-interactively: it reads stdin as context, processes the prompt argument, prints the result to stdout, and exits. Redirecting stdout to `RELEASE_NOTES.md` completes the CI workflow.

options.text: Use `claude --auto-approve` to skip all confirmations and run in the background
options.explanation: `--auto-approve` is not a recognised flag, and background execution does not address the non-interactive requirement.

questions.stem:
A company stores API secrets in environment variables. A developer configures an MCP server in `.mcp.json` with: `"apiKey": "sk-live-abc123"`. A security audit finds this a month later.

What went wrong and how should it be fixed?

options.text: The key was hardcoded instead of using `${API_KEY}` environment variable expansion; fix by replacing the hardcoded value with the expansion syntax
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` supports `${ENV_VAR}` expansion for secrets. The key should be `"apiKey": "${MY_API_KEY}"` where `MY_API_KEY` is set in the developer's environment. This keeps the secret out of the config file and out of version control.

options.text: The `.mcp.json` file should not be in the repo at all; delete it
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` is a legitimate project configuration file that should be version-controlled — but without hardcoded secrets.

options.text: MCP servers do not support API keys; the authentication method must be changed
options.explanation: MCP servers do support API key authentication; the issue is how the key is stored, not the authentication method.

options.text: The key was in the wrong JSON field; move it to the `secrets` array
options.explanation: `.mcp.json` does not have a `secrets` array; environment variable expansion is the correct mechanism.

questions.stem:
A bug classification pipeline labels issues as Critical, High, Medium, or Low. After deployment, the team finds that 60% of Medium issues are incorrectly classified as High. The system prompt says: 'High means the feature is broken for many users.'

Which system prompt change most directly fixes this?

options.text: Add 'Be conservative with High labels' to the system prompt
options.explanation: 'be conservative' is vague — it does not tell Claude where the High/Medium boundary is.

options.text: Define explicit quantitative criteria: 'High: >1,000 users affected with no workaround. Medium: 100-1,000 users OR a workaround exists' — with one example of each
options.explanation: Replacing vague definitions with explicit quantitative criteria gives Claude an unambiguous decision boundary. Including one example of each level further calibrates Claude on borderline cases.

options.text: Switch from four severity levels to two: Critical and Non-critical
options.explanation: Reducing to two categories loses the granularity the team needs to prioritise work.

options.text: Add a confidence score field and route any High-labeled issues above 0.8 confidence to human review
options.explanation: Routing based on confidence does not fix the underlying classification error — it adds overhead while the wrong boundaries remain.

questions.stem:
A team detects that their content moderation classifier is flagging 40% of legitimate cooking recipe posts as 'potentially harmful' due to language about cutting, slicing, and heat. The false positive rate is disrupting operations.

Which targeted fix addresses this without broadly reducing sensitivity?

options.text: Lower the classification threshold for all categories to reduce the overall false positive rate
options.explanation: Lowering the threshold for all categories reduces true positives as well as false positives, harming overall classifier effectiveness.

options.text: Remove the 'potentially harmful' category from the classifier entirely
options.explanation: Removing the category eliminates legitimate harmful content detection.

options.text: Add a domain-detection step: if the content is identified as a recipe, skip or adjust the harmful-content check for culinary terms
options.explanation: A domain-detection step applies context-aware logic: if the content is a recipe, culinary terms like 'cut' and 'heat' are harmless. This reduces false positives in the known problematic domain without broadly weakening detection.

options.text: Increase temperature so the model explores more classification options before committing
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases output variability, which worsens classification consistency.

questions.stem:
A pipeline extracts contract clauses and must include a `governing_law` field in every output. When the field is absent from the source document, Claude sometimes omits it from the output and sometimes hallucinates a value.

Which schema design prevents hallucination while ensuring the field is always present?

options.text: Mark `governing_law` as required in the schema but with no type constraint
options.explanation: A required field with no type constraint still pushes Claude to populate it with something, which may lead to hallucination when the value is absent.

options.text: Mark `governing_law` as an optional field that Claude can omit if not found
options.explanation: An optional field allows Claude to omit it, which the team does not want — the field must always appear.

options.text: Mark `governing_law` as required but nullable, with instructions to return null when the field is not present in the source
options.explanation: A required but nullable field combined with the instruction 'return null when not found' gives Claude a legitimate, non-hallucinated value to use when the source lacks the field. The field always appears in output (required), and null signals absence without fabricating data.

options.text: Add 'Never omit governing_law' to the system prompt
options.explanation: A text instruction to 'never omit' still leaves Claude with no sanctioned value when the field is genuinely absent, which can push it toward hallucination.

questions.stem:
A team calls the Message Batches API with 5,000 requests. Each request includes a `custom_id`. Twenty-four hours later, they retrieve the results. The results arrive in a different order than the requests.

How should the team correlate results to the original requests?

options.text: Sort results by timestamp — the API guarantees FIFO processing order
options.explanation: The Message Batches API does not guarantee FIFO processing order.

options.text: Use the `custom_id` field in each result to match it to the corresponding input request
options.explanation: `custom_id` is the designed correlation mechanism. Each result carries the same `custom_id` that was set in the corresponding input request, allowing the team to reliably match results to inputs regardless of processing order.

options.text: Re-submit the batch and specify `preserve_order: true`
options.explanation: `preserve_order: true` is not a parameter in the Message Batches API.

options.text: Infer order from the response content — each response includes the original request text
options.explanation: Results do not include the original request text — they reference the request only through `custom_id`.

questions.stem:
An agentic pipeline processes a financial report using multiple tool calls. Each tool returns a large JSON payload (~8KB). After 15 tool calls, the context window is nearly full and subsequent responses degrade in quality.

Which context management strategy best preserves task performance?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to extend how much Claude can output per turn
options.explanation: Max_tokens controls output length, not input (context) capacity.

options.text: Summarise or truncate each tool result before appending it to the conversation, retaining only the information needed for the current task
options.explanation: Trimming or summarising large tool results before appending them to the conversation dramatically reduces context growth. Retaining only the relevant subset of each 8KB payload keeps the context window sustainable across many tool calls.

options.text: Remove all previous tool results from the context and let Claude re-request them if needed
options.explanation: Removing all previous tool results forces Claude to re-invoke tools for information it has already retrieved, wasting latency and tokens.

options.text: Split the task across two separate Claude sessions and merge results manually
options.explanation: Splitting into two sessions creates a coordination problem and requires manual merging.

questions.stem:
A Claude integration has 35 tools available. Users report that Claude frequently calls the wrong tool — selecting `get_account_details` instead of `get_account_balance` for balance inquiries, and vice versa.

What is the most likely root cause?

options.text: 35 tools exceeds the maximum supported by the API; remove tools until fewer than 20 remain
options.explanation: 35 tools does not exceed an API limit, though it can increase cognitive load on the model.

options.text: The tool descriptions for `get_account_details` and `get_account_balance` are too similar; Claude cannot distinguish when to use each
options.explanation: When two tool descriptions have overlapping language — 'retrieves account information' vs. 'gets account data' — Claude struggles to determine which tool matches the user's intent. The fix is to make descriptions unambiguous: 'get_account_balance: Returns the current numeric balance and currency only' vs. 'get_account_details: Returns account metadata including owner name, creation date, and status — not the current balance.'

options.text: The tools are named too similarly; rename one to something completely different
options.explanation: Renaming without fixing the descriptions does not resolve the semantic overlap problem.

options.text: Claude ignores tool names and selects tools by their position in the list
options.explanation: Claude selects tools based on descriptions and names, not list position.

questions.stem:
An MCP server returns a 15,000-token JSON payload as a tool result. Claude's context window is 200,000 tokens. After the tool call, Claude's response degrades: it stops referencing earlier conversation turns and loses track of the original task.

What is the most effective fix?

options.text: Increase the context window limit to 500,000 tokens
options.explanation: The problem is not a hard context limit — at 15,000 tokens the window is not full. The issue is that a large tool result pushes earlier context further back, reducing its effective influence.

options.text: Ask Claude to process the tool result in a separate session
options.explanation: Using a separate session loses access to the original task context.

options.text: Have the MCP server return a compact summary of the key information rather than the full raw payload
options.explanation: A compact, structured summary from the MCP server delivers the essential information in far fewer tokens. This reduces context pressure and allows earlier task context to remain influential throughout the conversation.

options.text: Re-order the conversation so the tool result appears at the beginning of the context
options.explanation: Re-ordering a tool result before the conversation history is not possible without restructuring the conversation, and would destroy conversational coherence.

questions.stem:
A developer builds an MCP tool called `search_inventory` that returns up to 2,000 product records per call. Claude frequently exceeds context limits because the full record list is appended to the conversation.

Which tool design change most directly solves this?

options.text: Reduce the tool's result limit from 2,000 to 200 records
options.explanation: Reducing to 200 records reduces the problem but does not solve it architecturally — large results will still fill context for complex queries.

options.text: Change the tool to return paginated results with a `cursor` parameter and a `max_results` field that defaults to 20
options.explanation: Pagination with a configurable `max_results` default allows Claude to request small batches (e.g., 20 at a time), keeping each tool result small. Claude iterates with cursor values to retrieve subsequent pages when needed, rather than receiving a 2,000-record payload that floods the context.

options.text: Have the tool compress the records to base64 before returning them
options.explanation: Base64 encoding does not reduce token count — base64 is larger than the original text.

options.text: Return results as a resource URL that Claude can fetch separately instead of inline data
options.explanation: Returning a URL adds a fetch step and does not reduce the total data that eventually enters the context; it only defers the problem.
